brand.name: Fuster Cluck Farm
brand.tagline: A small family farm in Eastern North Carolina, raising pasture chicken, pork, and lamb the way it ought to be raised.

announce.message: Free delivery on orders $100+ in Johnston County · Next pickup Fri Apr 24

hero.subcopy: We're Jim and Carol, and we run a small family farm an hour east of Raleigh. Pasture chicken and pork, eggs from hens that live outside, sourdough baked on Friday — all of it brought up slow and sold direct.

homeStory.heading: We raise good food and honest animals.
body: It started with a handful of laying hens and a coop we built from scrap lumber. Fifteen years later we're still a small farm — by choice — because that's the only way we know how to do this well.
Our chickens live on pasture. Our pigs root through the woods. The sourdough comes out of our own oven. Nothing here is on a conveyor belt, and nothing here is pretending.

homeQuote.text: The chicken actually tastes like chicken. I didn't know what I was missing until I started buying from Jim and Carol.

homeCta.heading: Learn it with your own hands.
homeCta.body: From sourdough to backyard chickens — we host small workshops on the farm most weekends. Come get dirt under your fingernails.

classes.name: Sourdough from Scratch
classes.desc: Build a starter, learn the shaping, leave with a loaf.

classes.name: Backyard Chicken Basics
classes.desc: Breeds, coops, feed, predators. For first-time keepers.

classes.name: Butchering a Whole Chicken
classes.desc: Hands-on, take home what you process.

classes.name: Pasture Walk & Farm Tour
classes.desc: Meet the animals, see the rotations, ask anything.

animals.title: Meet the critters.
animals.lede: Everybody on this farm has a job — even the dogs. Here's who you'll find if you come visit.

sections.heading: Pasture first.
sections.body: The birds and pigs move to fresh grass on a rotation. That's better for the animals, better for the soil, and better for what ends up on your plate.

sections.heading: No junk.
sections.body: Non-GMO grain supplement to what they forage. No antibiotics, no hormones, no weird shortcuts.

sections.heading: Small batch.
sections.body: Poultry processed on-farm in small batches. Pork and lamb handled by a local USDA butcher we trust.

about.title: A small family farm with a funny name.
about.lede: Yes, the name is a pun. Yes, our neighbors made us explain it. We kept it anyway.
about.heading:
Fifteen years of
learning the hard way.
body: We started with a dozen chickens in the backyard in 2010. We wanted eggs we could trust, and one thing led to another. We built coops. We read every book. We made every mistake.

Today we raise pasture chicken, pork, and lamb on a piece of land in Kenly. We bake sourdough on Friday. We milk Jerseys for a pet-milk herdshare. We teach classes to anyone who wants to learn what we wish someone had taught us early.
We are not a big operation. We don't want to be. What we want is to raise good food for families who care where theirs comes from — and to know those families by name.

beliefs.heading: Small is the point.
beliefs.body: Every scale decision we make favors the animal, the soil, and the customer over growth.

beliefs.heading: Direct is honest.
beliefs.body: Selling straight to the family who eats it keeps us accountable in a way wholesale never does.

beliefs.heading: Teach what we know.
beliefs.body: We'd rather more people did this themselves than keep any of it secret.

classes.title: Workshops on the farm.
classes.lede: Small groups. Hands-on. Come ready to work and we'll send you home with something you made.

farmStore.title: The Farm Store.
farmStore.lede: Come pick out your cuts in person. The cooler's full on Friday morning and we restock as best we can through Saturday.
farmStore.coolerSub: Updated Friday morning. Call ahead — 919-632-1433 — if you want us to set something aside.

contact.title: Drop us a line.
contact.lede: Fastest way is a text. We'll get back to you between the morning chores and the evening feed — usually within a day.

gallery.title: Life on the farm.
gallery.lede: A little window into the everyday. Most of these come from our phones in our back pockets.
